slides.title: Why we test
body: Tests catch regressions before they hit production. Without them, every deploy is a guess.
Real example: Salesforce REQUIRES 75% Apex code coverage to deploy to production. No tests, no deploy. Stripe runs ~1 million automated tests on every PR — same idea, larger scale.

vocabulary.def: An Apex class marked @isTest that contains test methods. Doesn't count against your org's code limits.
Percentage of production code lines exercised by tests. Salesforce requires 75% to deploy.

slides.title: Test class anatomy
body: Mark the class with @isTest. Each test method is also marked @isTest (or named testMyThing). They take no params and return void.
Test classes don't count against your code-limit allotment — write all the tests you want.

slides.title: Your first test
slides.intro: Type this. Tests an imaginary Greeter.hello() method.
lineExplanations: @isTest at the class level marks the whole class as test-only.
@isTest at the method level marks this as a runnable test.
Test method: static, void, no params.
Call the method under test, capture the return.
assertEquals(expected, actual) — fails the test if they differ.

slides.title: Assertions
body: Use System.assertEquals(expected, actual, message) for value checks. System.assert(condition, message) for booleans.
Newer style: Assert.areEqual(...), Assert.isTrue(...). Both work; pick one and stay consistent.

vocabulary.def: A statement that fails the test if a condition isn't met. The basic check inside every test method.

slides.title: Multiple assertions
slides.intro: Type this. A single test can check multiple expectations.

slides.title: Test.startTest() / Test.stopTest()
body: Wrap the code under test in Test.startTest() / Test.stopTest(). It resets governor limits at the start and forces async jobs to run synchronously at stop.
Real example: testing async code? You MUST use start/stopTest, otherwise the queueable/future never runs in the test.

slides.title: startTest / stopTest
slides.intro: Type this. The wrapper makes governor accounting and async-execution behave predictably.
lineExplanations: Assert the record got an Id (proof it was inserted).

slides.title: @TestSetup
body: @TestSetup creates test data ONCE per class run. Each test method gets a fresh copy. Faster than re-creating in every method.
Real example: 50 tests all need 5 sample Accounts. With @TestSetup that's one creation; without it, that's 50 creations.

slides.title: Test data setup
slides.intro: Type this. Records are visible to every test method in the class.
lineExplanations: @TestSetup runs once before the test methods.
Setup method — must be static and void, takes no params.
Insert it. The record now exists for all tests.
Test method that queries the seeded data.
Query the lead created in setup.

slides.title: Negative tests
body: Test the unhappy path too. Use try/catch to assert that bad input throws an exception.
Real example: 'inserting a Lead without a LastName must fail' is just as important as 'a valid Lead inserts cleanly'. Both behaviors are part of the contract.

slides.title: Asserting an exception
slides.intro: Type this. The test passes only if an exception was caught.
lineExplanations: Create an INVALID lead (no required fields).
Wrap the code we expect to throw.
If we reach here, no exception fired — fail explicitly.
Reaching the catch is the success path.

slides.front: Why does Salesforce require 75% test coverage to deploy to production?
slides.back: Forces every team to ship at least minimal verification with their code, so changes can't silently break the org.
slides.detail: Coverage doesn't prove correctness — but zero coverage almost always means zero confidence. 75% is the industry's pragmatic floor.

slides.question: You're testing a method that fires off a future job. The test asserts something the future was supposed to do, but the assertion fails. What's missing?
slides.explanation: Test.startTest() / Test.stopTest() force async jobs (futures, queueables, batches) to actually execute by the time stopTest returns. Without it, the future never ran during the test.

slides.title: Capstone: Test class for CustomerService
slides.intro: Write a complete test class for the CustomerService.register method from Lesson 5 — a happy path that registers a valid customer and a negative path that rejects bad input. This is exactly the shape every Apex deploy needs to clear the 75% coverage gate.
lineExplanations: Class-level @isTest marks the whole class as test-only — doesn't count toward your code limit.
@TestSetup creates shared data once for the whole class run.
Setup method must be static, void, no params.
Insert it. Visible to every test method below.
Marks this method as a runnable test.
Test method: static, void, descriptive name.
Wraps the code under test. Resets governor limits at start; flushes async at stop.
Call the method under test, capture the return.
Assert the boolean result. Third arg is the failure message.
Negative path — bad email should return false.
Assert the rejection. No startTest/stopTest needed since there's no async.

slides.title: What you now know
body: @isTest classes and methods, assertions, Test.startTest/stopTest, @TestSetup, and negative tests.
Next: Governor Limits — the Salesforce-imposed caps that shape every Apex pattern you'll ever write.
